title: Will the Assurances of Former Prime Ministers Be Honoured?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: a duplicate OCR title fragment has been removed; the 1937 resistance reference, June 1963 meeting dates and damaged words in the agitation chronology have been verified from the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Every year, the movement observes the Day of Remembrance and Homage to the Martyrs of the Language Struggle. In every town in Tamil Nadu, DMK speakers recall the history of the movement's participation in that struggle. This year too, younger brother Ila. Pugazhendi, secretary of the Students' Wing, opened his statement announcing speakers across Tamil Nadu with a poem I wrote:
“Tamil, language of the charging bull,
Tamil, mountain rising from a sea of joy,
Tamil, flavour rising from the garden's leaf,
You climb through my body and my life and keep me living;
I bow to you.”
The year 2015 marks the fiftieth anniversary of the language struggle waged by the DMK in 1965, the golden jubilee of that history.

But the language struggle had begun earlier, in 1937-38. I was only fourteen, a student at Tiruvarur High School. When the Government ordered Hindi as a compulsory subject and required sufficient marks in it for promotion, all Tamil Nadu assumed the aspect of a battlefield. I set out as a schoolboy carrying a placard on which I had written:
“Come, everyone, let us go to the Hindi war;
Let us drive away the Hindi demon that has come!
Listen, Hindi woman who came running here!
This is not the cowardly land you sought.
In the land caressed by valiant Tamil,
We will not accept hollow words in our homes!”

Singing those lines, I led a procession of students through the streets of chariot-bearing Tiruvarur. That was the day I took my first step into politics and public life. It was also the day that made me a poet. My political journey, begun in 1938, continues at the age of ninety-two. With the same feeling, it will continue further.

The Constitution adopted in 1950 provided, by a margin of a single vote, that within fifteen years, by 1965, Hindi would take every place then occupied by English. The people of non-Hindi-speaking States repeatedly objected. The history of resistance that began in 1937 returned to Tamil Nadu in 1957. Like the Greeks who confronted each successive Persian invasion with greater strength, our movement has the distinction of stopping Hindi from entering Tamil Nadu with more strength, speed and resolve than in 1937.

For that reason, an Anti-Hindi Conference was held at Tiruvannamalai on 21 September 1957. Its proceedings and conclusions continue to strengthen all of us who resist Hindi today. Ascetic Arunagiri Adigal presided, C.V.M. Annamalai inaugurated it, and P.U. Shanmugam served as reception committee secretary. Perarignar Anna spoke longer there than at any other conference or event.

The movement's Executive Committee and General Council met on 8, 9 and 10 June 1963. To plan a prolonged campaign against Hindi, the General Council selected me to chair its Agitation Committee. Until then I had regarded my boundless happiness when Anna appointed me to lead the Kallakudi agitation at the 1953 Chidambaram conference as unsurpassable. But selection as chair of the Anti-Hindi Agitation Planning Committee submerged me in such happiness that I struggled even to thank the General Council.
The resolution concluded:

“Because the Official Languages Bill, Government orders and schemes intensify the imposition of Hindi, this General Council resolves that the DMK must launch direct action to protect our language rights, halt the invasive domination of Hindi, defeat the conspiracy to make us second-class citizens, and establish the rights of the people of the South. For this purpose, the General Council constitutes an Agitation Committee led by Comrade M. Karunanidhi and comprising Comrade N.V. Natarajan, district secretaries and State organisers.”
Do you know who proposed it? Our esteemed Professor Anbazhagan, who remains my younger elder brother and supports me to this day.

After the General Council, I spoke at the public meeting explaining its resolutions at the SIAA Grounds in Chennai. I began: “This is the most important struggle the DMK will conduct. Not merely our land but this subcontinent and the whole world eagerly await it. I regard selection to lead the committee created to shape, organise and deliberate upon this vast campaign as one of the greatest blessings of my life.”
At that meeting, Perarignar Anna sounded the drum:

“Our struggle will not begin on Friday and allow us to return home by Wednesday for an oil bath. Those who go to prison leaving a pregnant wife must not imagine they will return when the child is born and lying in its cradle. This struggle will be a continuing event lasting months and years.
“Enthusiasm alone is not enough. Enthusiasm mixed with haste is useless. Only those whose commitment will endure, whose courage will remain firm, and who can bear suffering and loss may take part.”

Our announcement produced such an effect that Lal Bahadur Shastri, speaking at an event in Hyderabad on 21 July, said: “DMK leaders must abandon their proposed anti-Hindi agitation. Their decision to oppose Hindi becoming the official language is deeply regrettable. Hindi will gradually remove English and take its place. Hindi is not a foreign language to us.”

Anna replied in a statement: “A bloodthirsty wolf lives in the ruling party's heart. The DMK is ready to give however much blood is needed to satisfy its hunger. Our agitation will test whether, after tasting our blood, its frenzy will subside and it will run away. We shall accept suffering and make every sacrifice.”

Anti-Hindi conferences were held in Salem on 4 August 1963, Thanjavur on 25 August and Tirunelveli on 22 September. At Tirunelveli, Anna proclaimed: “The train of agitation has departed. It will not stop midway while Hindi domination continues.”
At an event in Theni on 23 September, Anna presented me, as chair of the Agitation Committee, with a silver sword of valour and a shield. In praise, he said:

“For me, the unforgettable event in Karunanidhi's service is not only his labour for the Tiruvannamalai victory, nor his work to bring the Corporation into our hands. It was when, as a young man campaigning for our principles, members of the opposing party, enraged by his propaganda, beat and kicked him until they thought he was finished, savagely attacking him in Puducherry until blood gushed, imagining they had eliminated the bold youth who exposed the ruling party's excesses.

“Many colours may exist, but an artist is needed to create a fine painting from them. Likewise, a party may contain many kinds of people, but it needs those capable of making them work effectively. Younger brother Karunanidhi possesses those qualities. The fame he earned in cinema is no small thing. He won fame through *Parasakthi* and many later films. Had he trusted that fame and remained in that field, he could have lived with wealth and splendour. But he did not forget his ideal. Karunanidhi works without forgetting the movement or becoming intoxicated by fame.”
Can such words be forgotten?

As one stage of the righteous anti-Hindi struggle, DMK stalwart Singa Thamizhan Chinnasamy set himself alight and died in the open ground opposite Tiruchi railway junction. I unveiled a portrait of that flame of sacrifice at Tiruchi Devar Mandram on 6 December 1964.

Perarignar Anna was arrested at midnight on 25 January 1965. After completing a meeting at Kulithalai, I was travelling to attend one in Coimbatore when police intercepted and arrested me at Karur-Pasupathipalayam around one in the morning. Other leaders were arrested overnight. The DMK observed Republic Day that year as a day of mourning.

DMK comrade Sivalingam sacrificed his life in Chennai on 26 January 1965; comrade Aranganathan at Virugambakkam on the twenty-seventh; and Muthu at Keeranur. Students Rajendran and Ilangovan died from Government bullets fired upon students in Chidambaram.

Though we were released at the beginning of February, the sound of gunfire split Tamil Nadu's skies in the second week. Firing occurred at nine places in a single day. Trains were stopped, buses burned, telegraph wires cut and railway tracks uprooted. Mayavaram Sarangapani set himself alight and burned to death before his college. Ayyanpalayam Veerappan, Rangasamudram Muthu, Viralimalai Shanmugam and Peelamedu Dandapani must also be included in that roll.

The date 16 February 1965 is unforgettable. I was arrested in Chennai, taken by lorry to Palayamkottai and held in solitary confinement. News reached me there that Murasoli Maran had been arrested in Chennai under the Defence of India Act on 25 March.

Then I was told Anna was coming to see me in prison. Could I or the movement ever forget his visit and conversation, his words at a Tirunelveli meeting that day, “This place where my younger brother Karunanidhi lies in solitary confinement is now holy ground to which I must make pilgrimage,” or what he later wrote in *Kanchi* about meeting me? January 25, Homage Day, is the day to remember all this.
One could continue speaking of the anti-Hindi struggles of 1938 and 1965. Time alone does not permit it. It was because of our struggle that Prime Ministers made assurances in Parliament.

On 7 August 1959, Jawaharlal Nehru said: “There must be no imposition of Hindi. Second, for an indefinite and lengthy period, how long I do not know, I wish English to continue as an associate official language. I shall retain English as long as the people desire it. I shall not leave the decision that English should no longer continue to Hindi-speaking people. The non-Hindi-speaking people must decide.”

Again in Parliament in 1963, Nehru said no change in the position of English or Hindi should be made until the full consent of non-Hindi-speaking people had been obtained. Prime Minister Lal Bahadur Shastri gave the same assurance on 12 March 1965, and Indira Gandhi did so later.

Will today's Prime Minister Narendra Modi honour the assurance those three Prime Ministers gave the non-Hindi-speaking people? Or will he succumb to those pressing for the imposition of Hindi and Sanskrit? This year's Homage Day comes at a time when that fear is growing.
Our speakers at tomorrow's meetings must explain to the Tamil people that this year should become a day on which we insist that the Prime Minister give that assurance.
Will Prime Minister Narendra Modi honour the assurance of former Prime Ministers?
With affection,
M.K.
24 January 2015
